title: Saukville (town), Wisconsin

infobox.value: is a town in [Ozaukee County](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), United States. The [Village of Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin") is located in the town's southeast quadrant. The population was 1,755 at the 2000 census.

**Saukville** is a town in [Ozaukee County](/wiki/Ozaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Ozaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), United States. The [Village of Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin") is located in the town's southeast quadrant. The population was 1,755 at the 2000 census.

[Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee") and [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people") [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") lived in the area until the 1830s when the U.S. Federal Government forced them to leave Wisconsin. The first white settlers in the mid-1840s, and the Town of Saukville was organized in 1848. The early settlers were farmers, and dairy farming became the primary economic activity by the early 1900s. The Village of Saukville incorporated from some of the town's land in 1915, and while the village has become increasingly industrial, agriculture still plays a major role in the Town of Saukville's economy.

The town has thousands of acres of undeveloped, biodiverse [bogs](/wiki/Bogs "Bogs"), [coniferous swamps](/wiki/Coniferous_swamp "Coniferous swamp"), and primeval beech-maple forests. The largest of the community's natural areas is the 2,200-acre [Cedarburg Bog](/wiki/Cedarburg_Bog "Cedarburg Bog") State Natural Area. The town's bogs are a habitat for many endangered species, birds, and [carnivorous plants](/wiki/Carnivorous_plant "Carnivorous plant"). Among other landforms, the Cedarburg Bog contains a [string bog](/wiki/String_bog "String bog")—a geographic feature that seldom occurs as far south as Wisconsin—which contains many plant species rarely seen outside remote parts of Canada.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Saukville was the site of a [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") village at the crossroads of two [trails](/wiki/Great_Trail "Great Trail"), one of which became the north-south Green Bay Road and the other the east-west Dekora Road.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Also located on the banks of the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River"), the village's inhabitants were probably [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee") and [Sauk people](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The Native Americans were forced to leave Wisconsin in the 1830s, and white settlers arrived in the area around 1845 and began to build along Green Bay Road.

Saukville was part of the town of Port Washington until 1848 when the Town of Saukville was established. Throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries, Saukville was a rural community with many dairy farmers.[[5]](#cite_note-5) In 1871, a rail line was constructed through Saukville. It would eventually become part of the [Chicago, Milwaukee & St. Paul Railway](/wiki/Chicago,_Milwaukee,_St._Paul_and_Pacific_Railroad "Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad").

The Village of Saukville incorporated out of some of the Town of Saukville's land in 1915.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1973, the [Village of Newburg](/wiki/Newburg,_Wisconsin "Newburg, Wisconsin") incorporated from some of the Town of Saukville's land as well as land from the neighboring [Town of Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_Washington_County,_Wisconsin "Trenton, Washington County, Wisconsin") in Washington County.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Cedarburg_Beech_Woods.jpg/250px-Cedarburg_Beech_Woods.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cedarburg_Beech_Woods.jpg)

The Cedarburg Beech Woods State Natural Area west of the village contains an old-growth beech-maple forest, much like the ones that blanketed Saukville before settlers arrived in the 1800s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Ced-Bog_lake_June2013.jpg/250px-Ced-Bog_lake_June2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ced-Bog_lake_June2013.jpg)

The [Cedarburg Bog](/wiki/Cedarburg_Bog "Cedarburg Bog") State Natural Area contains many biodiverse wetlands with plant species seen in few other ecosystems.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the town has a total area of 33.3 square miles (86.4 km2), of which, 32.7 square miles (84.6 km2) of it is land and 0.7 square miles (1.8 km2) of it (2.04%) is water. The [Village of Saukville](/wiki/Saukville,_Wisconsin "Saukville, Wisconsin") is located in what was formerly the town's southeast quadrant. The town borders [Waubeka](/wiki/Waubeka,_Wisconsin "Waubeka, Wisconsin"), the [Town of Fredonia](/wiki/Fredonia_(town),_Wisconsin "Fredonia (town), Wisconsin"), and the [Village of Fredonia](/wiki/Fredonia,_Wisconsin "Fredonia, Wisconsin") to the north; the [Town of Port Washington](/wiki/Port_Washington_(town),_Wisconsin "Port Washington (town), Wisconsin") to the east; the [Town of Grafton](/wiki/Grafton_(town),_Wisconsin "Grafton (town), Wisconsin") and the [Cedarburg (town), Wisconsin](/wiki/Cedarburg_(town),_Wisconsin "Cedarburg (town), Wisconsin") to the south; and the [Town of Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_Washington_County,_Wisconsin "Trenton, Washington County, Wisconsin") and the [Village of Newburg](/wiki/Newburg,_Wisconsin "Newburg, Wisconsin") to the west.

The town is located in the [Southeastern Wisconsin glacial till plains](/wiki/Southeastern_Wisconsin_Till_Plains_(ecoregion) "Southeastern Wisconsin Till Plains (ecoregion)") that were created by the [Wisconsin glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsin_glaciation "Wisconsin glaciation") during the most recent ice age. The town has many glacial landforms, including [kames](/wiki/Kame "Kame"), [kettles](/wiki/Kettle_(landform) "Kettle (landform)"), and [moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine"). Much of the community is located on the [Racine Dolomite](/wiki/Racine_Dolomite "Racine Dolomite") formation that stretches through eastern Wisconsin and Illinois. The formation contains [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") marine fossils, and while much of the rock lies between 25 and 50 feet below the surface, the fossils of a prehistoric reef can be seen in some old quarries in the northern part of the Village of Saukville as well as in the 50-foot-high bluff along the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River") at Riveredge Nature Center.[[8]](#cite_note-Resources-8)

Before white settlers arrived in the area, the Saukville area was an upland forest dominated by [American beech](/wiki/Fagus_grandifolia "Fagus grandifolia") and [sugar maple](/wiki/Acer_saccharum "Acer saccharum") trees. There were also [white cedars](/wiki/Thuja_occidentalis "Thuja occidentalis") growing along the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River") and several thousand acres of [coniferous swamp](/wiki/Coniferous_swamp "Coniferous swamp") in Saukville's southwest quadrant. Much of the original forest—with the exception of the coniferous swamps—was cleared to prepare the land for agriculture. The Cedarburg Beech Woods State Natural Area in the western part of the town has old growth endemic trees with minimal damage from logging and retains the character of the pre-settlement beech-maple forests.[[8]](#cite_note-Resources-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Cedarburg_Bog.jpg/250px-Cedarburg_Bog.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cedarburg_Bog.jpg)

A network of streams, ponds and lakes flow through the Cedarburg Bog. Many of the Saukville area's bogs and swamps are flooded glacial [kettles](/wiki/Kettle_(landform) "Kettle (landform)") formed during the most recent ice age.

The town has many nature preserves and other undisturbed natural areas, the largest of which is the [Cedarburg Bog](/wiki/Cedarburg_Bog "Cedarburg Bog") State Natural Area, a 2,200-acre [National Natural Landmark](/wiki/National_Natural_Landmark "National Natural Landmark") with high biodiversity, which is home to many endangered plant and animal species. The largest and best preserved [bog](/wiki/Bog "Bog") in eastern Wisconsin, it contains two lakes, extensive white cedar and [tamarack](/wiki/Larix_laricina "Larix laricina") coniferous swamps, and the southernmost [string bog](/wiki/String_bog "String bog") on Earth.[[8]](#cite_note-Resources-8) The bog is a habitat for several [carnivorous plant](/wiki/Carnivorous_plant "Carnivorous plant") species, including [bladderworts](/wiki/Utricularia "Utricularia"), [pitcher plants](/wiki/Sarracenia_purpurea "Sarracenia purpurea"), and [round-leafed sundews](/wiki/Drosera_rotundifolia "Drosera rotundifolia").[[9]](#cite_note-Cedarburg_Bog_No._2-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service also considers the Cedarburg Bog to be an important habitat for the endangered [Hine's emerald](/wiki/Hine%27s_emerald "Hine's emerald") dragonfly.[[8]](#cite_note-Resources-8)

Other bogs in the town include [black spruce](/wiki/Picea_mariana "Picea mariana") swamps, which rarely occur at such southerly latitudes. Other bog plants that occur in Saukville include [leatherleaf](/wiki/Chamaedaphne "Chamaedaphne"), [snakemouth orchids](/wiki/Pogonia_ophioglossoides "Pogonia ophioglossoides"), [grass-pink orchids](/wiki/Calopogon_tuberosus "Calopogon tuberosus"), [bog-rosemary](/wiki/Andromeda_polifolia "Andromeda polifolia"), [winterberry](/wiki/Ilex_verticillata "Ilex verticillata"), [blueberry](/wiki/Blueberry "Blueberry"), and [cranberry](/wiki/Cranberry "Cranberry"). The town is also a habitat for the threatened [forked aster](/wiki/Eurybia_furcata "Eurybia furcata") plant and the [American gromwell](/wiki/Lithospermum_latifolium "Lithospermum latifolium"), which is a state-designated special concern plant species.[[8]](#cite_note-Resources-8)

As land development continues to reduce wild areas, wildlife is forced into closer proximity with human communities like Saukville. Large mammals, including [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer"), [coyotes](/wiki/Coyotes "Coyotes"), [North American river otters](/wiki/North_American_river_otter "North American river otter") and [red foxes](/wiki/Red_fox "Red fox") can be seen in the town. Many birds, including [great blue herons](/wiki/Great_blue_heron "Great blue heron") and [wild turkeys](/wiki/Wild_turkey "Wild turkey") are found in the town with the many swamps and bogs providing a habit birds such as the [osprey](/wiki/Osprey "Osprey").[[11]](#cite_note-glacial-11)

The region struggles with many invasive species, including the [emerald ash borer](/wiki/Emerald_ash_borer "Emerald ash borer"), [common carp](/wiki/Common_carp "Common carp"), [reed canary grass](/wiki/Phalaris_arundinacea "Phalaris arundinacea"), the [common reed](/wiki/Phragmites "Phragmites"), [purple loosestrife](/wiki/Lythrum_salicaria "Lythrum salicaria"), [garlic mustard](/wiki/Alliaria_petiolata "Alliaria petiolata"), [Eurasian buckthorns](/wiki/Rhamnus_(plant) "Rhamnus (plant)"), and [honeysuckles](/wiki/Honeysuckle "Honeysuckle").[[11]](#cite_note-glacial-11)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[12]](#cite_note-GR2-12) of 2000, there were 1,755 people, 622 households, and 518 families residing in the town. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 53.7 people per square mile (20.7/km2). There were 644 housing units at an average density of 19.7 per square mile (7.6/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 98.29% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.28% [Black](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.40% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 0.23% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.57% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 0.23% from two or more races. 1.31% of the population were [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic") or [Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") of any race.

There were 622 households, out of which 35.2% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 76.2% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 4.2% had a female householder with no husband present, and 16.6% were non-families. 12.9% of all households were made up of individuals, and 4.8% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.82 and the average family size was 3.11.

In the town, the population was spread out, with 26.5% under the age of 18, 5.5% from 18 to 24, 28.3% from 25 to 44, 28.4% from 45 to 64, and 11.3% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 40 years. For every 100 females, there were 107.9 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 102.2 males.

The median income for a household in the town was $60,435, and the median income for a family was $64,821. Males had a median income of $46,552 versus $31,406 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the town was $24,522. About 1.9% of families and 2.6% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including none of those under age 18 and 8.4% of those age 65 or over.

## Law and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Law and government")]

Saukville is organized as a [town](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Wisconsin#Town "Administrative divisions of Wisconsin") governed by an elected board, comprising a chairman and two supervisors. The current chairman is Kevin Kimmes.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

As part of [Wisconsin's 6th congressional district](/wiki/Wisconsin%27s_6th_congressional_district "Wisconsin's 6th congressional district"), the town is represented by [Glenn Grothman](/wiki/Glenn_Grothman "Glenn Grothman") (R) in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), and by [Ron Johnson](/wiki/Ron_Johnson "Ron Johnson") (R) and [Tammy Baldwin](/wiki/Tammy_Baldwin "Tammy Baldwin") (D) in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). [Duey Stroebel](/wiki/Duey_Stroebel "Duey Stroebel") (R) represents Saukville in the [Wisconsin State Senate](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Senate "Wisconsin State Senate"), and [Robert Brooks](/wiki/Robert_Brooks_(Wisconsin_politician) "Robert Brooks (Wisconsin politician)") (R) represents Saukville in the [Wisconsin State Assembly](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Assembly "Wisconsin State Assembly").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Education")]

The Town of Saukville is served by two [public school](/wiki/State_school "State school") districts: the joint [Port Washington-Saukville School District](/wiki/Port_Washington-Saukville_School_District "Port Washington-Saukville School District") and the [Northern Ozaukee School District](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Ozaukee_School_District&action=edit&redlink=1 "Northern Ozaukee School District (page does not exist)"), which also covers Fredonia, Newburg, and parts of the Town of Belgium.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Students in the Port Washington-Saukville School District attend Saukville Elementary School for kindergarten through fourth grade, Thomas Jefferson Middle School for fifth through eighth grades, and [Port Washington High School](/wiki/Port_Washington_High_School_(Wisconsin) "Port Washington High School (Wisconsin)") for ninth through twelfth grades.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Students in the Northern Ozaukee School District attend Ozaukee Elementary School for kindergarten through fifth grade, Ozaukee Middle School for sixth through eighth grades, and Ozaukee High School for grades nine through twelve.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Additionally, the Riveredge School is a tuition-free, public elementary [charter school](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school") authorized by the Northern Ozaukee School District. Located at the Riveredge Nature Center in the northwestern Town of Saukville near the municipal boundary with the Village of Newburg, the school serves children from kindergarten through fifth grade.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee") Field Station is a 320-acre nature preserve and laboratory adjacent to the [Cedarburg Bog](/wiki/Cedarburg_Bog "Cedarburg Bog") State Natural Area in the town. The field station grounds and laboratories are not open to the general public, but are used by university students and faculty to conduct biological and ecological research.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[Interstate 43](/wiki/Interstate_43 "Interstate 43") passes through the southeastern part of the Town of Saukville with Exit 96 servicing the Village of Saukville. [Wisconsin Highway 57](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_57 "Wisconsin Highway 57") and [Wisconsin Highway 33](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_33 "Wisconsin Highway 33") also pass through the town.

Saukville has limited public transit compared with larger cities. Ozaukee County and the [Milwaukee County Transit System](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Transit_System "Milwaukee County Transit System") run the Route 143 commuter bus, also known as the "Ozaukee County Express," to Milwaukee via Interstate 43. The bus stops in the Saukville [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart") parking lot, near I-43 Exit 96. The stop is the route's northern terminus. The bus operates Monday through Friday with limited hours corresponding to peak commute times.[[20]](#cite_note-Wisconsin_DOT-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Ozaukee County Transit Services' Shared Ride Taxi is the public transit option for traveling to sites not directly accessible from the interstate. The taxis operate seven days a week and make connections to Washington County Transit and Milwaukee County Routes 12, 49 and 42u.[[20]](#cite_note-Wisconsin_DOT-20)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The [Wisconsin and Southern Railroad](/wiki/Wisconsin_and_Southern_Railroad "Wisconsin and Southern Railroad") operates a freight line passing through the eastern part of the town.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The community currently does not have a passenger train station.

## Nature preserves, parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Nature preserves, parks and recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Ced-Bog_Kiosk_June2013.jpg/250px-Ced-Bog_Kiosk_June2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ced-Bog_Kiosk_June2013.jpg)

Hikers can access the 2,200-acre Cedarburg Bog via a network of trails and boardwalks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Ced-Bog_pier_June2013.jpg/250px-Ced-Bog_pier_June2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ced-Bog_pier_June2013.jpg)

The boardwalk at the Cedarburg Bog extends into Mud Lake and provides opportunities for birdwatching.

### State natural areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: State natural areas")]

* **Cedarburg Beech Woods**: The Cedarburg Beech Woods is a 74-acre nature preserve owned by the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee, containing tamarack swamps and old-growth beech-maple forests dating to the time before white settlers cleared much of Ozaukee County's forests for agriculture.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
* **[Cedarburg Bog](/wiki/Cedarburg_Bog "Cedarburg Bog")**: The Cedarburg Bog State Natural Area is a 2,200-acre [National Natural Landmark](/wiki/National_Natural_Landmark "National Natural Landmark") with high biodiversity. The area has hiking trails with views of the largest and best preserved [bog](/wiki/Bog "Bog") in eastern Wisconsin and a boardwalk and birdwatching platform on Mud Lake in the center of the preserve.[[9]](#cite_note-Cedarburg_Bog_No._2-9)
* **Riveredge Creek and Ephemeral Pond**: The Riveredge Nature Center—a private, nonprofit education facility—maintains a 61-acre nature preserve rich in plant and insect life near the confluence of Riveredge Creek and the Milwaukee River. The nature center contains glacial landforms typical of the [Kettle Moraine](/wiki/Kettle_Moraine "Kettle Moraine") region and 50-foot-high [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") dolomite bluffs along the river.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[8]](#cite_note-Resources-8)
* **Sapa Spruce Bog**: The Sapa Spruce Bog is a 24-acre nature preserve owned by the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee. Located in a flooded glacially formed kettle, the bog occurs farther south than other black spruce-tamarack bogs typically do. The Sapa Spruce Bog is more acidic than other bogs in the region, creating a unique ecosystem of flora and fauna—including six species of [Sphagnum](/wiki/Sphagnum "Sphagnum") moss—not typically seen so far south.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Ozaukee County Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ozaukee County Parks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Pioneer_Village_9198.jpg/250px-Pioneer_Village_9198.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pioneer_Village_9198.jpg)

An [historical reenactment](/wiki/Historical_reenactment "Historical reenactment") at Ozaukee County Pioneer Village.

* **Ehlers Park**: A narrow seven-acre park on the east bank of the Milwaukee River, Ehlers Park has 2,200 feet of river front with canoe launches and fishing spots.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* **Hawthorne Hills Park, H. H. Peters Youth Camp, and Ozaukee County Pioneer Village**: Located on the Milwaukee River in northeastern Saukville, these three, sandwiched-together county parks offer a wide range of activities. Hawthorne Hills Park features 57 acres of undeveloped forest with hiking trails on the river as well as an 18-hole golf course. The adjacent H. H. Peters Youth Camp is an additional 40 acres with hiking trails liked to Hawthorne Hills and a camp hall with showers and restrooms. Pioneer Village is directly north of the Hawthorne Hills Golf Course and is an [open-air museum](/wiki/Open-air_museum "Open-air museum") that preserves twenty-four buildings from the 1840s through the early 1900s, providing a snapshot of village life in early Ozaukee County.[[29]](#cite_note-29)
* **Tendick Nature Park**: The 122-acre Tendick Nature Park has a boardwalk through wetlands, a canoe launch on the Milwaukee River, an archery range, and picnic facilities.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Ozaukee Washington Land Trust

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Saukville_(town),_Wisconsin&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ozaukee Washington Land Trust")]

* **Shannon Preserve**: A 34-acre nature preserve with hardwood forests, cattail swamps, and a lake, the Shannon Preserve has hiking trails and opportunities for birdwatching.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Additionally, the Blue Heron Wildlife Sanctuary on the Milwaukee River is a private, nonprofit nature center with 92-acres of land, hiking trails, and [wildlife rehabilitation](/wiki/Wildlife_rehabilitation "Wildlife rehabilitation") facilities. [North American river otters](/wiki/North_American_river_otter "North American river otter") have been spotted from the sanctuary's trails.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The Town of Saukville is also home to The Bog, a private, 18-hole golf course located east of the Cedarburg Bog.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
